publication_timeline.note: Bars show dated rows only. Blank line segments mean the current public corpus does not expose a usable year for that point.

artist.title: Artist lens
artist.summary: This is a market-shape read, not a songwriting oracle. In the classified slice, Pop carries the strongest share of visible attention, the strongest duration pocket is 3–4 min, and collaboration helps most when it expands reach without making the track anonymous.
artist.question: Where does the field still leave room for a release to feel distinct instead of merely legible to an existing machine?

next_moves.label: Study release archetypes
next_moves.note: See the distinct ways tracks reached the field before turning one winner into a universal template.

next_moves.label: Test a hypothetical upload
next_moves.note: Change duration, packaging, tags, and channel size to see what moves the percentile story.

next_moves.label: Walk the source rows
next_moves.note: Drop back to the songs themselves before borrowing a pattern into a real release decision.

artist.takeaway: The practical question is not which genre is supposedly 'best.' It is whether your release shape can still find oxygen once attention narrows around a few familiar patterns.

consumer.title: Consumer lens
consumer.summary: Listeners are moving through a field that is both concentrated and partially labeled. The visible publication window spans 2009 to 2026, but only 28% of rows carry public year data, which means breadth has to be read with some caution.
consumer.question: Is this field genuinely broad, or does it only feel broad because the same corridor keeps repainting itself in new colors?

next_moves.label: Trace the tag universe
next_moves.note: See whether discovery is wide or still circling inside a tight tag neighborhood.

next_moves.label: Read the bias surface
next_moves.note: Check who gets the front row once language, genre, and collaboration are counted together.

next_moves.label: Inspect the public rows
next_moves.note: Stay close to the songs when the discovery story starts sounding too polished.

consumer.takeaway: A healthy discovery system should feel broader than its hit list. If the surface feels repetitive, the numbers suggest you may not be imagining it.

business.title: Business lens
business.summary: Attention remains narrow enough that a small set of channels can still steer the visible market. The top 5 hold 12.5% of views and the top 10 hold 19.1%, which is useful if you need to separate repeatable distribution logic from one-off mythology.
business.question: Which signals still move the market after inherited scale is discounted, and which ones only look strong because the room was preloaded?

next_moves.label: Pressure-test predictability
next_moves.note: Separate controllable lift from folklore by checking what the ensemble can explain.

next_moves.label: Inspect raw vs partial signals
next_moves.note: See which correlations survive after channel size stops dominating the picture.

next_moves.label: Read the cultural field
next_moves.note: Move into corridor balance, inheritance pressure, and authenticity posture before productizing the conclusion.

business.takeaway: The value is not 'which song went viral.' It is which conditions keep narrowing or opening the market across multiple cohorts.

research.title: Research lens
research.summary: The useful question is not whether a pattern exists somewhere, but whether it survives classification gaps, coverage limits, and cohort changes. This lane is strongest when it keeps the leaderboard separate from the broader structural context instead of pretending they are the same thing.
research.question: Which claims shrink under coverage pressure, and which ones stay sharp enough to survive disagreement?

next_moves.label: Audit the dated field
next_moves.note: Check timeline gaps, known-year share, and whether a temporal story is carrying more certainty than the rows allow.

next_moves.label: Inspect the row layer
next_moves.note: Keep the conclusion close to the source rows instead of repeating an abstracted summary.

next_moves.label: Open the methods surface
next_moves.note: Cross-check assumptions, quality gates, and contract posture before treating the output as settled.

research.takeaway: The method earns trust when the story gets smaller where coverage is weak, and sharper where multiple checks agree.

interpretation: Analysing 636 public songs across multiple datasets. Known-gender coverage is 21% and known-genre coverage is 87%, so the equity read should be treated as directional rather than total. Within the labeled subset, gender parity is 0.98 and female artists average 463,818,200 views/song versus 734,129,344 for male artists (ratio 0.63). Genre concentration is high overall (Gini 0.525), while the classified-only genre diversity reads 0.455. The top 5 channels still control 12.5% of all views.
